This sum was therefore included as a matrimonial asset. For APAX Tech Pte Ltd's $100,000 paid-up capital, an adverse inference was drawn against the husband for concealment, with $30,000 (after accounting for the $70,000 previously added to the $503,882 sum) included in the pool. The home theatre sound system was valued at $2,500 using a broad-brush approach. The husband's total assets were determined at approximately $2,598,492.

cases.case_name: Lin Tze Kin (suing as co-executor of the estate of Tan Ah Kar, deceased) and another v Lim Sze Eng and another
cases.parties: Claimants: Lin Tze Kin and Tan Yen Lin Alice (co-executors of Tan Ah Kar's estate). Defendants: Lim Sze Eng and Tan Lay Hoon.
cases.court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore
cases.judgment:
The court found that the defendants failed to prove the existence of an alleged 1993 agreement whereby they purchased the property from TAK for $570,000. TAK retained beneficial ownership of her 50% share in the property, which she validly bequeathed in her will. The court ordered the property to be sold with the defendants having a right of first option to purchase TAK's half-share. The defendants are entitled to equitable accounting for improvements made to the property from 2007-2021, excluding the 1998 works which were not proved to have been paid by them.

TAK and her husband LWC were joint owners of a property purchased in the 1960s-1970s as their family home. Upon LWC's death in 1992, TAK became sole owner. The defendants claim they purchased the property from TAK for $570,000 in 1993 after TAK struggled to pay the overdraft facility secured on the property. In 2008, TAK transferred 50% of the property to the defendants as a gift while retaining the other 50% as tenant-in-common. TAK died in 2023 and bequeathed her 50% share to her claimant son and his sons. The defendants also counterclaimed for $1.5 million allegedly entrusted to TAK for safekeeping, and disputed TAK's contribution to two investment units purchased in 2001.

The judgment addresses the principles of equitable accounting where co-owners make improvements to jointly-owned property, establishing that a party cannot take the benefit of property value increases without making allowance for what the other co-owner expended to achieve that increase. The case demonstrates the importance of documentary evidence in establishing oral family agreements, particularly regarding substantial financial transactions. The court's finding that TAK had capacity to make a valid will in 2012 and the weight given to her contemporaneous statements regarding property ownership establish important precedent on testamentary capacity and hearsay evidence from deceased persons.
cases.holding: The court found that the purported 1993 agreement for the defendants to purchase the property for $570,000 never existed. The defendants failed to discharge their burden of proof as there was no documentary evidence supporting payment of $570,000, and D1's explanations regarding funds from Taiwan property sales and banking slips were unreliable and contradictory. The court found D1 orchestrated the registration of the 3/6/08 Notice of Death to enable acquisition of adjoining land for redevelopment, after which he caused TAK to transfer a half-share to the defendants without consideration. D1's conduct over 14 years before seeking transfer, coupled with TAK's continued reference to 'her' half-share in recordings and her 2012 will (made when she had good cognitive capacity), demonstrated TAK beneficially owned her half-share until death. The court found recordings of TAK reliable as she only suffered cognitive decline from 2020 onwards. The court ordered the property sold and granted defendants a right of first option to purchase TAK's half-share. The court found defendants entitled to equitable accounting for improvements made from 2007-2021 (totalling approximately $701,475) but rejected claims regarding 1998 works as unpaid by defendants. The court found no valid trust relationship regarding the $1.5 million claimed by D1, as evidence showed these were TAK's own funds or funds from other sources. The court found no evidence TAK had a beneficial interest in the FEP Units, as the $150,000 contribution was TAK's own money, not from D1's entrusted sums.

cases.case_name: Equation Recycling Pte Ltd v Leading Bio-Energy (S) Pte Ltd
cases.parties: Appellant: Equation Recycling Pte Ltd (tenant/landlord). Respondent: Leading Bio-Energy (S) Pte Ltd (user/tenant).
cases.case_ref: [2026] SGHC 126, District Court Appeal No 23 of 2025
cases.court: General Division of the High Court of Singapore (Sushil Nair JCA)
cases.judgment:
The High Court allowed the appeal in part. The Court found that the respondent should not have been permitted to advance the unpleaded penalty argument, but the appellant's victory on this point was pyrrhic as the Court found the DJ erred in interpreting the forfeiture clause. The Court held that while the law of penalties may apply to security deposits under leases, the forfeiture clause in this case did not amount to an unenforceable penalty as the appellant failed to demonstrate actual losses.

Equation Recycling Pte Ltd (appellant) leased a service area to Leading Bio-Energy (S) Pte Ltd (respondent) under a Handling Service Agreement from June 2021 to December 2023 for $40,000 monthly rent. The respondent paid a refundable security deposit of $120,000 (three months' rent). The respondent breached the agreement by failing to pay rent and utilities from August 2022. After multiple default judgments and a settlement agreement in October 2023, the respondent vacated the premises in December 2023. When the respondent demanded return of the security deposit, the appellant refused, claiming the right to forfeit it under Recital 4 of the agreement for breaches. The respondent subsequently sued for recovery of the deposit.

This case establishes important principles regarding the distinction between the law of penalties and the law of deposits in Singapore law. The Court clarifies that while the law of penalties may apply to forfeiture of security deposits under leases (unlike purchase deposits), the application depends on whether the deposit has an earnest function. The decision also addresses the interplay between the law of deposits originating from civil law and the common law penalty doctrine, and confirms that procedural fairness principles require material facts to be pleaded even if precise legal labels are not required.
cases.holding: The judge held that while the respondent failed to plead the penalty argument in its statement of claim, it should be permitted to advance it as an unpleaded argument because: (1) it was a legal issue arising from the appellant's reliance on Recital 4; and (2) no irreparable prejudice was occasioned. On the merits, the judge found that Recital 4 imposed secondary obligations on the respondent to pay compensatory damages for breaches, bringing it within the scope of the penalty rule. The security deposit of $120,000 was not a genuine pre-estimate of loss since it could be forfeited for any breach, including minor breaches causing lesser loss. Accordingly, the forfeiture clause was an unenforceable penalty. The judge rejected the appellant's arguments based on Li Jialin and Hon Chin Kong as inapplicable to security deposits under leases as opposed to purchase deposits. The judge further held that the Settlement agreement had compromised all pre-settlement breaches and that the appellant had failed to establish legal or factual basis for set-off of claimed losses against the deposit.

cases.case_name: Zhuang Yijie v Openmeta Pte Ltd and others
cases.parties: Applicant: Zhuang Yujie. Respondents: (1) Openmeta Pte Ltd (2) Aster Foundation Ltd (3) Huang Feiling
cases.court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore
cases.judgment:
The application was allowed with costs to be paid by Huang to Zhuang. Zhuang was granted permission to commence a derivative action in the name of Openmeta Pte Ltd and Aster Foundation Ltd against Huang Feiling for alleged breaches of fiduciary duties and related claims. Zhuang was authorized to have full charge and control over the conduct of any such proceedings and execution proceedings thereafter.

Zhuang Yujie and Huang Feiling are co-founders of Openmeta Pte Ltd and Aster Foundation Ltd, both incorporated in Singapore in 2021 to operate a Web3 domain name sales business. The companies raised 950,000 USDT through token issuance in November 2021, with the funds received into cryptocurrency wallets controlled solely by Huang. Disputes arose concerning alleged misappropriation of 591,050 USDT to Wangsan (a company 99% owned by Huang), disappearance of 150 ETH attributed to alleged security breach, unauthorized sale of approximately 941.63 ETH and unaccounted-for 515.96 ETH, removal of Zhuang's access to a jointly-managed wallet containing 143.52 ETH, and alleged unauthorized use of the "Star Protocol" brand for airdrop campaigns and $ANS token listing. Huang maintained the assets belonged to Web3 Investments and were held in his personal capacity for operational convenience.

This decision clarifies the procedural requirements and substantive principles governing statutory derivative actions under Section 216A of the Companies Act 1967 (Singapore). The court emphasized that leave stage applications should maintain proportionality to avoid protracted proceedings, applying a low evidentiary standard where only obviously unmeritorious claims are culled. The judgment provides guidance on assessing good faith (requiring honest belief in cause of action and absence of predominantly collateral purpose) and prima facie company interests (requiring reasonable semblance of merit and practical commercial benefit), while acknowledging that mere factual disputes between warring founders do not establish bad faith without evidence of vendetta clouding judgment.
cases.holding: The court allowed Zhuang's application for leave to commence a derivative action under section 216A. The court found that Zhuang satisfied all four requirements: he had the requisite standing as a shareholder of Openmeta and as a proper person in relation to Aster; he provided the requisite 14 days' notice; he was acting in good faith; and the intended actions were prima facie in the interests of the Companies. The court rejected Huang's arguments that the Companies had suffered no losses. Regarding good faith, the court found that although a collateral purpose may exist (relating to XStar Identity), the applicant was genuinely aggrieved and the collateral purpose was sufficiently consistent with doing justice to the Companies. Regarding the prima facie interests of the Companies, the court found the individual claims had a reasonable semblance of merit and were not frivolous or vexatious, including the alleged misuse of fundraised funds, the loss of ETH, unauthorized conversions, removal of Joint Wallet access, misuse of the Star Protocol brand, and failure to keep accounts. The court applied a low evidential standard and did not adjudicate on the disputed facts.

Zhuang was authorized to have full charge and control over the conduct of any such proceedings and execution proceedings thereafter.
